behaviors.description: The speech-to-text output preserves speaker labels so that the summary can attribute statements to specific attendees.
behaviors.justification: A unit test with a mocked STT API would hardcode the response format and pass, hiding real failures where the actual STT API returns a different structure (e.g., 'speaker_id' instead of 'speaker', or missing speaker fields entirely). The real failure boundary is: does the deserializer correctly handle the actual STT API's response format? This requires testing the transcription module integrated with real or cached API response data to catch format mismatches that a pure mock would hide.
behaviors.test_description: Load a realistic meeting transcript with real STT API response data (either from a cached fixture matching the live API format or by calling the real API), pass it through the transcription deserialization module, and verify that speaker labels are correctly extracted and remain available in the structured summary for downstream attribution logic.

behaviors.description: The structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who was assigned a task during the meeting.
behaviors.justification: A mocked LLM would always return action items for all attendees by design, hiding the real failure: the LLM doesn't understand task assignments in natural language well enough to generate items for every attendee. This is quality-dependent, non-deterministic behavior—different LLM runs or models may capture different subsets of task assignments. Only a real LLM call on realistic transcripts can verify that the summarization pipeline actually produces action items for each assigned task.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a realistic meeting transcript (with multiple attendees and explicit task assignments like 'Alice will handle the client outreach, Bob will set up the infrastructure') to the full summarization pipeline with a real LLM. Verify that the structured summary contains at least one action item for every attendee who received a task assignment, and that each item correctly identifies the assignee.

behaviors.description: Key decisions mentioned in the transcript appear in the "decisions" section of the summary, not buried in the narrative.
behaviors.justification: Mocking the LLM with a pre-structured response would always pass, hiding the real failure: the LLM doesn't correctly identify and extract decisions from narrative content, or places them in the narrative instead of the decisions section. This is a quality issue—whether the LLM successfully recognizes decision statements and categorizes them correctly depends on the real model's language understanding. Only a real LLM call can verify this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Provide a realistic meeting transcript containing explicit decisions (e.g., 'We decided to launch in Q2', 'We agreed to use Postgres instead of MongoDB') mixed with narrative and action items. Call the real LLM summarization pipeline and verify that the 'decisions' section of the returned summary contains these decisions, not action items or narrative paraphrases.

behaviors.description: When the audio file is longer than 60 minutes, the service splits it into segments and processes them sequentially, merging the results into a single coherent summary.
behaviors.justification: A unit test with mocked segment summaries would always merge correctly, hiding real failures in the merge logic—e.g., losing speaker context between segments, duplicating decisions, or creating incoherent narrative flow. The real failure is coordination: does the merge logic correctly maintain coherence across multiple realistic segment summaries? This requires integration testing with realistic multi-segment transcripts to catch merge failures that mocks would hide. (Note: System-level testing with a real 60+ minute audio file might be needed if the STT API has specific behavior for long files, but Integration captures the core split/merge coordination issue.)
behaviors.test_description: Create or use cached transcripts from a real long audio file split into sequential segments (e.g., three 30-minute segments from a 90-minute meeting). Pass each segment through the transcription and LLM summarization pipeline, then feed the per-segment summaries to the merge logic. Verify that the final merged summary maintains speaker continuity, preserves key decisions without duplication, and reads as a coherent whole without gaps or contradictions.

behaviors.description: The full pipeline — upload audio, transcribe, summarize, deliver structured output — completes without manual intervention for a real meeting recording.
behaviors.justification: The behavior explicitly tests end-to-end pipeline completion with a real meeting recording. The real failures are orchestration-level: API timeouts, format mismatches between steps, coordination failures, or delivery failures. Testing individual steps (Unit, Integration, Agentic) would miss failures that only emerge when all components run together in sequence. The pipeline requires real infrastructure (real audio file, real STT API, real LLM, real output storage/delivery) and multiple coordinated steps, which is the definition of Workflow-level testing.
behaviors.test_description: Upload a real meeting recording (or a realistic test recording) to the service, invoke the full pipeline (audio upload → STT transcription → LLM summarization → structured output delivery), and verify that a complete, valid structured summary with all required fields (decisions, action items, attendee attributions) is produced and delivered without any manual intervention, step failures, API timeouts, or format mismatches.
